i started running without phone or headphones to focus even more on what was happening on my body

and through the most intense part of todays run i suddenly realized that i had been thinking for like 20 minutes about the insights i was having while running, and how i would write them to share them here

it’s always fascinating to capture myself on that. the mind is just incessant in its pursuit of approval. “omg this is the best insight ever i will think it through all this time and write it on my head and have it ready to write it when i arrive to share it with farcaster and become a little bit more accepted by my internet frens”

just bullshit. as soon as i realized that i made a focused decision to come back to my body, breath and practice, and give up that insight. I dont even remember it. and it doesnt matter. the only thing that actually matters is developing the capacity to be present

and the mind is so good at disguising presence with incessant thought. even on the most extreme situations

First year to winter running (Montreal), and ran a half marathon Monday. LFG.

Some thoughts from my morning run.

I smoked for most of 16 years. Cigs, weed, shisha, cigars, vapes… you name it.

Spent my 20s moonlighting as a DJ/producer, playing shows, traveling, partying till sunrise.

Needless to say, my body’s been through some shit.

Running scratches the same masochistic itch (minus the long-term damage).

It’s one of the few sports where progress is guaranteed if you just keep moving.

One foot in front of the other. That’s it.

Each step takes you further from your origin and closer to your destination.

Each form correction, a quiet reminder: you can always adjust, always improve.

And yet—it never gets easier.
You just get better at enduring.

Maybe that’s why I stuck with it.
A solitary ritual. A moving meditation. A metaphor that runs deep.

What a story!!
I meet this couple most of the time I go out running. They always walk together no matter the weather (met them in winter and now with 30+ degrees C)

Today I stopped by to introduce myself and get to know them better. 

He is 88 and she is 85 😱

He still drive a car. She is brilliant, was joking about her age and surname. They never stopped doing sports. They once were amateur cross country skiers. 

I want to be like them when I’m old. 

“Mens sana in corpore sano”

I was vibing all the time talking to them. They transmit the joy of living. I smiled for 8 km going back home.

They greeted me with: “never stop doing what you are doing”. 

If this does not transmit you the motivation to ignite the discipline to workout, I don’t know what would do it.

processedSessions.text: Saturday run under the sun ☀️

New Shoe:

Saucony Endorphin Elite II

Now that I’ve been cleared to run fast again, if figured it’s time to try a new super shoe and see if I can buy some extra speed. 

For $290 it really needs to do something special. 220 grams for a men’s Us size 11. Super light and the craziest feeling foam I’ve ever had on my feet. Super squishy and soft when trying to walk or even run slowly. The shoe just feels super bouncy, unstable, but fast and fun at the same time. It’s kind of what I imagine it’s like to break a wild bronco would be like. 

I only put in 3 miles, and the jury is still out. I like them, but not 100% sure I love them yet. The foam is definitely noticeable, but I really don’t feel the typical snappiness of the carbon plated shoe. 

Has anyone else been trying any of the new super shoes that are available?

cast.text:
perhaps the core insight that life has brought to me is the role of *balance* in allowing the unleashing of the spirit through the human experience. i just came back from my training session of today and i had to strive for a cadence of 180, while keeping the pace at 4:50 min/km. this was hard, because usually that cadence made me go at ~4:15

so the mission of today was to go slower, but move my feet faster. striving for that balance between the two. and it went great. i feel very good about it

and trusting my coach @satzo7 in the process. first we train cadence. once mastered we train speed

the year was 2017. i went to travel the world on my own and started with something i *really* wanted to do:

go to a roger waters concert. I decided that it would be on amsterdam, and started my trip there. got some psychedelic truffles on a store that was called “elements of nature”, and then headed to the concert on my own

it was fundamentally epic. and today she came to my awareness as i was running

who is she?

the girl that i saw after the concert ended. i stayed standing in the same place where I was throughout the concert with my eyes closed

sinking and feeling what just had happened

and then after what felt like 20 minutes i opened my eyes and saw her

she was about 30. crying her heart out of her eyes. my instinct was to go towards her and just hug her. i hugged her for a long time, as she said to me

“no one cares”

and i said to her

“and no one will ever will”

i had her through my running today because i felt those words inside

1/

My name is Clayton, and this is my running journey.

I didn’t run growing up. I’m a late bloomer. 

By 24 years old and in 2011, I had run only a handful of 5ks. 

In 2012, I started boxing and ran my first ever 10ks.

I discovered Obstacle Course Races the same year and completed the first Tough Mudder Colorado, instantly enjoying OCRs and trails more than street running.

When I turned 30 in 2017, I set a target of running 100 5ks in the year. 

I accomplished that target and reached a new distance of half marathons. 

In 2018, I ran my first marathon around Lake Okoboji in Iowa. 

I enjoyed the distance, but not the pavement.

That led me to look for trail races. I stumbled onto Ultra Trail Marathons, signing up for a 50k that October.

I completed that ultramarathon, but I came in literal last place.

it’s always fascinating to capture myself on that. the mind is just incessant in its pursuit of approval. “omg this is the best insight ever i will think it through all this time and write it on my head and have it ready to write it when i arrive to share it with farcaster and become a little bit more accepted by my internet frens”

just bullshit. as soon as i realized that i made a focused decision to come back to my body, breath and practice, and give up that insight. I dont even remember it. and it doesnt matter. the only thing that actually matters is developing the capacity to be present

and the mind is so good at disguising presence with incessant thought. even on the most extreme situations

Some thoughts from my morning run.

I smoked for most of 16 years. Cigs, weed, shisha, cigars, vapes… you name it.

Spent my 20s moonlighting as a DJ/producer, playing shows, traveling, partying till sunrise.

Needless to say, my body’s been through some shit.

Running scratches the same masochistic itch (minus the long-term damage).

It’s one of the few sports where progress is guaranteed if you just keep moving.

One foot in front of the other. That’s it.

Each step takes you further from your origin and closer to your destination.

Each form correction, a quiet reminder: you can always adjust, always improve.

And yet—it never gets easier.
You just get better at enduring.

Maybe that’s why I stuck with it.
A solitary ritual. A moving meditation. A metaphor that runs deep.

cast.text:
perhaps the core insight that life has brought to me is the role of *balance* in allowing the unleashing of the spirit through the human experience. i just came back from my training session of today and i had to strive for a cadence of 180, while keeping the pace at 4:50 min/km. this was hard, because usually that cadence made me go at ~4:15

so the mission of today was to go slower, but move my feet faster. striving for that balance between the two. and it went great. i feel very good about it

and trusting my coach @satzo7 in the process. first we train cadence. once mastered we train speed

the year was 2017. i went to travel the world on my own and started with something i *really* wanted to do:

go to a roger waters concert. I decided that it would be on amsterdam, and started my trip there. got some psychedelic truffles on a store that was called “elements of nature”, and then headed to the concert on my own

it was fundamentally epic. and today she came to my awareness as i was running

who is she?

the girl that i saw after the concert ended. i stayed standing in the same place where I was throughout the concert with my eyes closed

sinking and feeling what just had happened

and then after what felt like 20 minutes i opened my eyes and saw her

she was about 30. crying her heart out of her eyes. my instinct was to go towards her and just hug her. i hugged her for a long time, as she said to me

“no one cares”

and i said to her

“and no one will ever will”

i had her through my running today because i felt those words inside

1/

i wouldn’t have thought 2 months ago that I would be running 10k for a training session

but here we are. the target was 5:30/km, and as soon as i started i got the takeaway for today:

better to start slower and get used to the pace as the session progresses

i started too fast. i had also calculated the amount of seconds needed for 250m, and soon i discovered that it was better to get up with the pace with 100m intervals. 33 seconds each. that was way more helpful to find the pace

and then after getting it i started aiming for 66 seconds every 200m. that felt like it clicked

it’s amazing how it works once you catch up the pace. this is my first time running at this one. good session
